title: Fungal Skin Infections (Tinea / Dermatophytosis)
oneLiner: Superficial fungal infection of skin/hair/nails caused by dermatophytes; common in all ages, easily treated but often misdiagnosed or under-treated.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – do NOT dismiss if the “tinea” is rapidly spreading, extremely painful out of proportion, with bullae, crepitus, or systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, altered GCS). *Key finding: severe pain + rapid progression + systemic illness.*
- **Cellulitis/erysipelas with sepsis** – especially if the patient is immunocompromised (diabetes, HIV, chemotherapy) and has a spreading, warm, tender rash with fever. *Key finding: lymphangitic streaking, firm induration, fever >38°C.*
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms
  - **Itching** (variable – can be marked, especially in tinea pedis and tinea cruris)
  - **Annular (ring-shaped) rash** with a raised, scaly, advancing border and central clearing
  - **Hair loss** (tinea capitis – patchy, broken hairs, scaling, ± kerion – boggy, inflamed plaque)
  - **Nail changes** (onychomycosis – thickened, yellow/brown, crumbling, subungual debris)
  - **Interdigital maceration and fissuring** (tinea pedis – athlete’s foot)
- Key exam findings
  - Well-demarcated scaly plaques with raised borders
  - Central clearing (especially in tinea corporis)
  - Size range: small coin-sized patches to large confluent areas
  - Kerion: tender, boggy, pus-discharging nodule on scalp (can cause scarring alopecia)
  - Wood’s lamp: some species (e.g., *Microsporum canis*) fluoresce apple-green; most do not
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → Less likely, with 1-line differentiators**
  - **Nummular eczema** – scattered, well-defined plaques but no central clearing; often more heavily scaly; not annular
  - **Psoriasis** – salmon-pink plaques with silvery scale; typical sites (elbows, knees, scalp); nail pitting not crumbling
  - **Pityriasis rosea** – herald patch followed by “Christmas tree” distribution on trunk; central clearing much less consistent
  - **Erythema migrans (Lyme disease)** – single expanding red lesion with central clearing; history of tick bite; no scale
  - **Granuloma annulare** – annular dermal papules, no scale; often on dorsal hands/feet; asymptomatic
  - **Contact dermatitis** – acute onset with vesiculation, weeping, no central clearing; history of exposure
- **Splitter:** Potassium hydroxide (KOH) microscopy from skin scrapings – positive for septate hyphae = tinea; negative = eczema/psoriasis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - **Skin scrapings** from the active border of the lesion (scrape with #15 scalpel blade onto glass slide)
  - **KOH preparation** – 10-20% KOH drop onto scrapings, coverslip, gently heat; look for branching hyphae under microscope (x10, x40)
  - **Wood’s lamp** – only useful for tinea capitis due to *M. canis*; most common cause (*T. tonsurans*) does not fluoresce
- **Bloods:**
  - Generally not indicated for uncomplicated tinea
  - Consider **FBC, CRP, glucose** if immunocompromised or suspected secondary bacterial infection
- **Imaging:**
  - Not needed
- **Special tests:**
  - **Fungal culture** (Sabouraud agar) – for treatment-resistant cases, identification of species, or confirmation when KOH negative but suspicion high; takes 2-4 weeks
  - **Skin biopsy** – only if atypical or to exclude other conditions
- **Never-miss test:** **KOH microscopy** – omitting this risks treating eczema/psoriasis as fungal syndrome or prescribing oral antifungals unnecessarily.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / primary care):**
  - **Topical antifungal** for localised tinea corporis, cruris, pedis – no need to wait for culture
  - **For tinea capitis or extensive tinea:** arrange oral antifungal – do not treat with topicals alone
  - **Rule out serious mimics** (see Killers First) – if any red flags (severe pain, systemic toxicity, rapid spread, bullae) → escalate immediately
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Rarely required unless immunocompromised with disseminated infection or secondary bacterial superinfection (manage cellulitis per local guidelines)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Tinea corporis/cruris/pedis:** topical antifungal × 2-4 weeks (until 1 week after clearing)
  - **Tinea capitis:** oral antifungal × 4-8 weeks (terbinafine or griseofulvin); topical alone ineffective
  - **Onychomycosis:** oral antifungal × 6-12 weeks (fingernails) or 12-24 weeks (toenails); topical less effective but can be tried for superficial distal disease
  - **Adjunctive measures:** keep skin dry, avoid tight synthetic clothing, disinfect shoes/footwear, treat household contacts if symptomatic (especially tinea capitis)
- **Default Orders (if admitted for secondary bacterial infection):**
  - IV fluids if septic: 0.9% saline at 500 mL over 30 minutes (then reassess)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if not contraindicated)
  - Observations: NEWS2 hourly initially if sepsis; otherwise 4-hourly
  - Diet: normal
  - Wound care: daily dressing with emollient / antimicrobial if superinfected
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Topical terbinafine 1% cream** – apply OD-BD for 1-2 weeks (e.g., tinea corporis) or 2-4 weeks (tinea pedis)
  - **Topical clotrimazole 1% cream** – apply BD for 2-4 weeks (alternative)
  - **Oral terbinafine** – adults: 250 mg PO OD; children: weight-based (see BNF); duration: 2-4 weeks for tinea corporis/pedis, 4-8 weeks for tinea capitis
  - **Oral griseofulvin** – adults: 500 mg-1 g PO daily in divided doses (microsize); children: weight-based; duration: 6-12 weeks (tinea capitis)
  - **Oral itraconazole** – adults: 100 mg PO OD (or 200 mg BD for pulse therapy); check capsule dose (capsules better absorbed with food); duration: 1-2 weeks for tinea corporis/pedis, 4-8 weeks for nail
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Topical ketoconazole, miconazole, or econazole (equally effective)
  - Oral fluconazole (less active against dermatophytes; only for some *Candida*)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Terbinafine: caution with CYP2D6 substrates (e.g., tricyclic antidepressants, SSRIs, beta-blockers); avoid in active/lupus
  - Itraconazole: strong CYP3A4 inhibitor – do not use with statins (except pravastatin), warfarin, some benzodiazepines
  - Griseofulvin: reduces warfarin effect; avoid in porphyria
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Terbinafine: avoid if eGFR <30 mL/min (or reduce dose – [check local policy])
  - Itraconazole: avoid in hepatic impairment
  - Griseofulvin: avoid in severe hepatic disease
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Griseofulvin with warfarin** – INR will drop; monitor and adjust warfarin. **Itraconazole with statin** – risk of rhabdomyolysis. **Terbinafine overdose** – rarely serious but can cause hepatic injury.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Secondary bacterial cellulitis (especially tinea pedis – portal of entry for *Strep. pyogenes*)
  - Kerion (tinea capitis) – inflammatory reaction leading to scarring alopecia
- **Chronic:**
  - Scarring alopecia (especially from kerion or long-standing tinea capitis)
  - Nail destruction, onychomycosis with pain, difficulty walking
  - Disseminated dermatophytosis (rare; in severely immunocompromised – HIV, transplant)
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent in immunocompetent patients – complete cure with appropriate therapy. Without treatment, tinea persists for years and can spread. Recurrence common (20-30% with tinea pedis) due to reinfection or inadequate treatment.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **KOH scrapings before oral treatment** – confirm diagnosis and avoid unnecessary systemic antifungals in eczema/psoriasis.
- **Treat tinea capitis with oral antifungals** – topical alone never cures hair follicle infection.
- **Review household contacts for tinea capitis** – treat asymptomatic carriers to prevent reinfection; check for fungal organisms on scalp using a fine-toothed comb.
- **In tinea pedis, treat shoes and socks** – spray with antifungal powder or replace to avoid reinfection.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Applying topical corticosteroids alone** – will suppress inflammation transiently but allow fungal spread; can cause “tinea incognito” (atypical appearance without typical scale).
- **Using topical antifungals for tinea capitis** – a wasted effort; must give oral therapy.
- **Stopping treatment too early** – many patients stop when itch resolves; must continue until 1 week after clinical cure (usually 2-4 weeks total).
- **Ignoring tinea pedis as a complication risk** – breaks in skin predispose to lower-limb cellulitis; treat promptly in patients with diabetes.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening redness, pain, swelling, or fever suggestive of secondary bacterial infection
  - Spread of rash despite 2 weeks of treatment
  - Development of boggy scalp lesion (kerion) – may need drainage and oral steroids
  - If on oral terbinafine/griseofulvin: new jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, or abdominal pain (hepatotoxicity – rare but urgent)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Review in 2-4 weeks to assess response
  - If tinea capitis: culture at 4 weeks – if positive, consider resistance; repeat course or change agent
  - Onychomycosis: follow-up at 3-6 months for clinical and mycological cure
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ID/dermatology if:**
  - Suspected disseminated dermatophytosis in immunocompromised (e.g., HIV, solid organ transplant)
  - Treatment failure after 2 adequate courses
  - Kerion not resolving with oral antifungals (consider systemic steroids)
  - Severe secondary bacterial infection (cellulitis with sepsis) – use NEWS2; if ≥5, escalate per local sepsis protocol
- **ICU involvement:**
  - Only if septic shock from secondary bacterial infection (not from tinea itself)
  - Criteria: persistent hypotension despite fluids, lactate >4 mmol/L, new organ dysfunction

rawMarkdown:
# Fungal Skin Infections (Tinea / Dermatophytosis)
**One-liner:** Superficial fungal infection of skin/hair/nails caused by dermatophytes; common in all ages, easily treated but often misdiagnosed or under-treated.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – do NOT dismiss if the “tinea” is rapidly spreading, extremely painful out of proportion, with bullae, crepitus, or systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, altered GCS). *Key finding: severe pain + rapid progression + systemic illness.*
- **Cellulitis/erysipelas with sepsis** – especially if the patient is immunocompromised (diabetes, HIV, chemotherapy) and has a spreading, warm, tender rash with fever. *Key finding: lymphangitic streaking, firm induration, fever >38°C.*

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms
  - **Itching** (variable – can be marked, especially in tinea pedis and tinea cruris)
  - **Annular (ring-shaped) rash** with a raised, scaly, advancing border and central clearing
  - **Hair loss** (tinea capitis – patchy, broken hairs, scaling, ± kerion – boggy, inflamed plaque)
  - **Nail changes** (onychomycosis – thickened, yellow/brown, crumbling, subungual debris)
  - **Interdigital maceration and fissuring** (tinea pedis – athlete’s foot)
- Key exam findings
  - Well-demarcated scaly plaques with raised borders
  - Central clearing (especially in tinea corporis)
  - Size range: small coin-sized patches to large confluent areas
  - Kerion: tender, boggy, pus-discharging nodule on scalp (can cause scarring alopecia)
  - Wood’s lamp: some species (e.g., *Microsporum canis*) fluoresce apple-green; most do not

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → Less likely, with 1-line differentiators**
  - **Nummular eczema** – scattered, well-defined plaques but no central clearing; often more heavily scaly; not annular
  - **Psoriasis** – salmon-pink plaques with silvery scale; typical sites (elbows, knees, scalp); nail pitting not crumbling
  - **Pityriasis rosea** – herald patch followed by “Christmas tree” distribution on trunk; central clearing much less consistent
  - **Erythema migrans (Lyme disease)** – single expanding red lesion with central clearing; history of tick bite; no scale
  - **Granuloma annulare** – annular dermal papules, no scale; often on dorsal hands/feet; asymptomatic
  - **Contact dermatitis** – acute onset with vesiculation, weeping, no central clearing; history of exposure
- **Splitter:** Potassium hydroxide (KOH) microscopy from skin scrapings – positive for septate hyphae = tinea; negative = eczema/psoriasis.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - **Skin scrapings** from the active border of the lesion (scrape with #15 scalpel blade onto glass slide)
  - **KOH preparation** – 10-20% KOH drop onto scrapings, coverslip, gently heat; look for branching hyphae under microscope (x10, x40)
  - **Wood’s lamp** – only useful for tinea capitis due to *M. canis*; most common cause (*T. tonsurans*) does not fluoresce
- **Bloods:**
  - Generally not indicated for uncomplicated tinea
  - Consider **FBC, CRP, glucose** if immunocompromised or suspected secondary bacterial infection
- **Imaging:**
  - Not needed
- **Special tests:**
  - **Fungal culture** (Sabouraud agar) – for treatment-resistant cases, identification of species, or confirmation when KOH negative but suspicion high; takes 2-4 weeks
  - **Skin biopsy** – only if atypical or to exclude other conditions
- **Never-miss test:** **KOH microscopy** – omitting this risks treating eczema/psoriasis as fungal syndrome or prescribing oral antifungals unnecessarily.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / primary care):**
  - **Topical antifungal** for localised tinea corporis, cruris, pedis – no need to wait for culture
  - **For tinea capitis or extensive tinea:** arrange oral antifungal – do not treat with topicals alone
  - **Rule out serious mimics** (see Killers First) – if any red flags (severe pain, systemic toxicity, rapid spread, bullae) → escalate immediately
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** Rarely required unless immunocompromised with disseminated infection or secondary bacterial superinfection (manage cellulitis per local guidelines)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Tinea corporis/cruris/pedis:** topical antifungal × 2-4 weeks (until 1 week after clearing)
  - **Tinea capitis:** oral antifungal × 4-8 weeks (terbinafine or griseofulvin); topical alone ineffective
  - **Onychomycosis:** oral antifungal × 6-12 weeks (fingernails) or 12-24 weeks (toenails); topical less effective but can be tried for superficial distal disease
  - **Adjunctive measures:** keep skin dry, avoid tight synthetic clothing, disinfect shoes/footwear, treat household contacts if symptomatic (especially tinea capitis)
- **Default Orders (if admitted for secondary bacterial infection):**
  - IV fluids if septic: 0.9% saline at 500 mL over 30 minutes (then reassess)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if not contraindicated)
  - Observations: NEWS2 hourly initially if sepsis; otherwise 4-hourly
  - Diet: normal
  - Wound care: daily dressing with emollient / antimicrobial if superinfected

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Topical terbinafine 1% cream** – apply OD-BD for 1-2 weeks (e.g., tinea corporis) or 2-4 weeks (tinea pedis)
  - **Topical clotrimazole 1% cream** – apply BD for 2-4 weeks (alternative)
  - **Oral terbinafine** – adults: 250 mg PO OD; children: weight-based (see BNF); duration: 2-4 weeks for tinea corporis/pedis, 4-8 weeks for tinea capitis
  - **Oral griseofulvin** – adults: 500 mg-1 g PO daily in divided doses (microsize); children: weight-based; duration: 6-12 weeks (tinea capitis)
  - **Oral itraconazole** – adults: 100 mg PO OD (or 200 mg BD for pulse therapy); check capsule dose (capsules better absorbed with food); duration: 1-2 weeks for tinea corporis/pedis, 4-8 weeks for nail
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Topical ketoconazole, miconazole, or econazole (equally effective)
  - Oral fluconazole (less active against dermatophytes; only for some *Candida*)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Terbinafine: caution with CYP2D6 substrates (e.g., tricyclic antidepressants, SSRIs, beta-blockers); avoid in active/lupus
  - Itraconazole: strong CYP3A4 inhibitor – do not use with statins (except pravastatin), warfarin, some benzodiazepines
  - Griseofulvin: reduces warfarin effect; avoid in porphyria
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Terbinafine: avoid if eGFR <30 mL/min (or reduce dose – [check local policy])
  - Itraconazole: avoid in hepatic impairment
  - Griseofulvin: avoid in severe hepatic disease
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Griseofulvin with warfarin** – INR will drop; monitor and adjust warfarin. **Itraconazole with statin** – risk of rhabdomyolysis. **Terbinafine overdose** – rarely serious but can cause hepatic injury.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Secondary bacterial cellulitis (especially tinea pedis – portal of entry for *Strep. pyogenes*)
  - Kerion (tinea capitis) – inflammatory reaction leading to scarring alopecia
- **Chronic:**
  - Scarring alopecia (especially from kerion or long-standing tinea capitis)
  - Nail destruction, onychomycosis with pain, difficulty walking
  - Disseminated dermatophytosis (rare; in severely immunocompromised – HIV, transplant)

### Prognosis
- Excellent in immunocompetent patients – complete cure with appropriate therapy. Without treatment, tinea persists for years and can spread. Recurrence common (20-30% with tinea pedis) due to reinfection or inadequate treatment.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **KOH scrapings before oral treatment** – confirm diagnosis and avoid unnecessary systemic antifungals in eczema/psoriasis.
- **Treat tinea capitis with oral antifungals** – topical alone never cures hair follicle infection.
- **Review household contacts for tinea capitis** – treat asymptomatic carriers to prevent reinfection; check for fungal organisms on scalp using a fine-toothed comb.
- **In tinea pedis, treat shoes and socks** – spray with antifungal powder or replace to avoid reinfection.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Applying topical corticosteroids alone** – will suppress inflammation transiently but allow fungal spread; can cause “tinea incognito” (atypical appearance without typical scale).
- **Using topical antifungals for tinea capitis** – a wasted effort; must give oral therapy.
- **Stopping treatment too early** – many patients stop when itch resolves; must continue until 1 week after clinical cure (usually 2-4 weeks total).
- **Ignoring tinea pedis as a complication risk** – breaks in skin predispose to lower-limb cellulitis; treat promptly in patients with diabetes.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening redness, pain, swelling, or fever suggestive of secondary bacterial infection
  - Spread of rash despite 2 weeks of treatment
  - Development of boggy scalp lesion (kerion) – may need drainage and oral steroids
  - If on oral terbinafine/griseofulvin: new jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, or abdominal pain (hepatotoxicity – rare but urgent)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Review in 2-4 weeks to assess response
  - If tinea capitis: culture at 4 weeks – if positive, consider resistance; repeat course or change agent
  - Onychomycosis: follow-up at 3-6 months for clinical and mycological cure

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ID/dermatology if:**
  - Suspected disseminated dermatophytosis in immunocompromised (e.g., HIV, solid organ transplant)
  - Treatment failure after 2 adequate courses
  - Kerion not resolving with oral antifungals (consider systemic steroids)
  - Severe secondary bacterial infection (cellulitis with sepsis) – use NEWS2; if ≥5, escalate per local sepsis protocol
- **ICU involvement:**
  - Only if septic shock from secondary bacterial infection (not from tinea itself)
  - Criteria: persistent hypotension despite fluids, lactate >4 mmol/L, new organ dysfunction
